paper_title: Absorbing Complexity: An Interaction-Native Knowledge Harness for Financial LLM Agents

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Financial AI agents often fail for a simple reason: they make users carry the complexity. A user must repeatedly restate goals, risk preferences, portfolio context, past judgments, and shifting market assumptions, while the agent answers, retrieves, acts, and forgets. In finance, this is not just inconvenient. In tasks such as market analysis, copy-trading review, and trade preparation, forgotten context and stale memory can create latency, repeated errors, weak auditability, and unsafe decisions. We propose the interaction-native knowledge harness (InKH), an architecture for financial LLM agents that absorbs complexity into the system. InKH converts user, market, portfolio, and tool events into structured operational knowledge. It uses passive knowledge injection to assemble a bounded working context buffer before the main model step, temporal graph memory for low-latency retrieval, a wiki audit surface for human-readable governance, and background extraction with maturity, decay, and write-time invalidation. We evaluate InKH on a reproducible controlled synthetic benchmark with 24 random seeds, 4 rounds, 80 episodes per round, and 6 baselines, producing 46,080 baseline-conditioned evaluations. InKH achieves mean task quality of 0.815 at 900 ms latency. Compared with agent-driven wiki-walk memory, it reduces latency by 82.95 percent, token cost by 82.29 percent, and stale-knowledge usage by 96.58 percent, while improving quality by 0.108 and traceability by 0.461. Compared with a temporal-graph system without invalidation, it improves quality by 0.050 and reduces stale-memory usage by 96.58 percent with comparable serving cost. The results support a design thesis for financial AI: adoption happens when complexity is absorbed by the system rather than transferred to the user. The benchmark validates architecture-level behavior, not live trading performance.

sections.title: Start
sections.content: Absorbing Complexity: An Interaction-Native Knowledge Harness for Financial LLM Agents 6 2 0 2 1 ] . [ 1 6 8 8 1 0 . 6 0 6 2 : r True Trading AI Ailiya Borjigin True Trading ailiya.borjigin@gmail.com Igor Stadnyk True Trading igor@true.trading Ben Bilski True Trading ben@true.trading Maksym Chikita Inc4.net m.chikita@inc4.net Dmytro Kyrylenko Inc4.net dmitriy.k@inc4.net Sofiia Pidturkina Inc4.net s.pidturkina@inc4.net Julia Stadnyk Inc4.net julia.b@inc4.net Abstract Problem. Financial AI adoption is constrained not only by model quality but by financial cognition friction: users must repeatedly restate fragmented information, historical judgments, risk preferences, and evolving market assumptions. Existing financial agents remain largely turn-based and workflow-disposable: they answer, retrieve, act, and forget. In high-stakes settings such as market analysis, copy-trading evaluation, and trade preparation, this leads to latency, repeated error, stale-memory reuse, and weak traceability. Approach. We propose the interaction-native knowledge harness (InKH), financial-agent architecture that absorbs complexity into the system rather than transferring it to the user. The architecture combines: (i) an event-stream view of user, market, and tool updates; (ii) bounded working context buffer assembled by passive knowledge injection rather than agent-driven memory search; (iii) temporal knowledge graph as the low-latency retrieval substrate; (iv) wiki audit surface for human-readable governance; and (v) background extraction, maturity, decay, and write-time invalidation. Results. We report reproducible controlled benchmark with 24 seeds, 4 rounds, 80 episodes per round, and 6 baselines, for 7,680 workflows per baseline and 46,080 baselineconditioned evaluations overall. InKHachieves mean task quality 0.815 at 900 ms mean latency. Relative to an agent-driven wiki-walk memory, InKHreduces latency by 82.95%, token cost by 82.29%, and stale-knowledge usage by 96.58%, while improving task quality by 0.108 and decision traceability by 0.461. Relative to an otherwise similar temporal-graph system without invalidation, InKHimproves quality by 0.050 and reduces stale-memory usage by 96.58% with comparable serving cost. 1 Impact. The results support central design claim: adoption happens when complexity is absorbed by the system rather than transferred to the user. In financial AI, this means continuously transforming interaction traces into structured, persistent, and operational knowledge, while keeping execution safety and auditability explicit.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Large language model agents are gradually moving from one-shot question answering toward sustained financial workflows. In practice, these workflows include market analysis, portfolio review, trader evaluation, risk checking, order preparation, and user confirmation. Yet most systems still behave as turn-based assistants: the user asks, the agent retrieves, the agent answers, and most of the useful context disappears afterward. This creates recurring latency, duplicated reasoning, fragile personalization, and repeated rediscovery of the same risks. Our central argument is that adoption happens when complexity is absorbed by the system rather than transferred to the user. In financial AI, users should not have to manually coordinate fragmented information, historical judgments, risk preferences, and changing market assumptions. Instead, production-grade system should continuously convert interaction traces into structured, persistent, and operational knowledge. We call the associated reduction in user burden financial cognition friction. This paper proposes the interaction-native knowledge harness (InKH), new architecture for continuous financial cognition. The design draws inspiration from interaction-native AI systems that treat collaboration as continuous rather than turn-based [1], from compiled-knowledge approaches such as the LLM Wiki pattern [2], and from recent work on scalable agent memory, experiential learning, graph-backed memory, and financial agent benchmarks [8, 12, 14, 15, 17, 19, 2124]. The paper is also intentionally complementary to recent work on execution-layer safety in agentic finance. Borjigin et al. argue that in agentic crypto trading, execution is the new attack surface and propose survivability-aware local executors to enforce non-bypassable last-mile constraints [25]. Related work by Borjigin and He develops constrained execution and auditable compliance layers for cross-market trade execution [26]. Taken together, those works address the downstream action plane; this paper addresses the upstream cognition plane. Safe financial agents require both. The contributions of this paper are fourfold: 1. We propose InKH, an interaction-native architecture for continuous financial cognition built from passive knowledge injection, bounded working context buffer, temporal graph memory, and wiki audit surface. 2. We formalize the architecture with explicit state, knowledge objects, retrieval utility, injection, decay, invalidation, maturity transition, and governance constraints. 3. We provide algorithms for passive injection, background extraction, and maintenance, together with implementation guidance for graph+wiki systems, entity matching, upsert semantics, and latency budgeting. 4. We report reproducible experimental study on controlled financial benchmark and show that InKHmaterially improves quality, latency, stale-memory suppression, repeated error reduction, and traceability relative to memory and non-memory baselines.

sections.title: 2 Literature Review
sections.content: Interaction-native collaboration recent technical report from Thinking Machines Lab argues that current AI systems suffer from communication bottleneck because they alternate between discrete input and output phases rather than collaborating continuously [1]. Their proposed interaction models natively process overlapping streams of audio, video, and text, and use real-time foreground model together with asynchronous background reasoning. This paper adopts the architectural lesson rather than the multimodal training recipe: financial cognition should be continuous and stateful, even when operationalized over text and event streams rather than full-duplex speech or video. Compiled knowledge, memory, and graph-backed retrieval Karpathys LLM Wiki pattern proposes an appealing separation between immutable raw sources, LLM-maintained wiki pages, and explicit ingest/query/lint operations [2]. In parallel, the memory literature has advanced rapidly. MemGPT frames multi-tier memory management as an operatingsystem problem [11]. Mem0 emphasizes production-ready long-term memory and reports large latency and token improvements over full-context baselines [17]. A-MEM pushes further toward dynamically organized agentic memory [18]. Zep/Graphiti introduces temporal knowledge graph memory layer with explicit validity windows and relationship-aware retrieval [19], while recent survey synthesizes graph-based memory around extraction, storage, retrieval, and evolution [20]. InKH is positioned at different layer than Graphiti. Graphiti provides temporal validity windows and relationship-aware retrieval at the storage layer, whereas InKH operates at the orchestration layer: retrieval is passive and system-injected rather than agent-requested, and invalidation is performed at write time during background extraction rather than only at query time. InKH further adds governance layer that gates which knowledge may influence which financial actions based on maturity and action risk. InKH is therefore not replacement for Graphiti; production implementation could use Graphiti or similar temporal graph system as the underlying substrate. Our position is that finance needs both compiled knowledge and governed graph retrieval : the graph should serve online retrieval, while the wiki remains the audit surface. Experience accumulation and self-improvement Several influential papers show that agents can improve without finetuning by learning from their past trajectories. Reflexion introduces verbal reinforcement and episodic reflection [8]. ExpeL extracts reusable knowledge from set of training experiences [14]. Voyager accumulates reusable skills through an expanding code library [15]. Generative Agents and Self-Refine likewise demonstrate the importance of reflection, memory, and iterative improvement in interactive systems [7, 9]. Self-RAG adds adaptive retrieval and self-critique [10]. These works support the broader claim that past experience should become future capability, but they do not address the governance and temporal invalidation demands of financial reasoning. Financial agents, retrieval, and benchmarks Finance-specific agent research remains comparatively sparse. FinMem is notable early effort that builds layered-memory trading agent with profiling and decision modules [12]. Benchmarking work has accelerated more recently. FinanceBench shows that open-book financial QA remains difficult even for strong retrieval-augmented systems [21]. Finance Agent Benchmark tests real-world research 3 tasks involving SEC filings and expert-authored questions [22]. BizFinBench extends benchmark coverage to business-driven financial tasks [24]. FinAgentBench targets agentic retrieval in financial question answering [23]. These studies clarify what current systems still miss: not only domain knowledge, but robust multi-step retrieval and repeated, governed adaptation. Execution, governance, and financial agent stacks Recent financial-agent papers by Borjigin and collaborators provide an important complementary perspective. Execution Is the New Attack Surface formalizes survivability-aware execution middleware for agentic crypto trading [25]. Safe and Compliant Cross-Market Trade Execution adds constrained reinforcement learning, action shielding, and auditable compliance [26]. Our paper extends that trajectory upstream: before actions are made survivable, the agent must maintain the right evolving financial state. Classical retrieval and tool-using agents The proposed design also builds on standard retrieval and tool-use literature. Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) formalized non-parametric memory augmentation [3], RETRO showed retrieval at enormous scale [4], ReAct unified reasoning and acting [5], and Toolformer demonstrated selfsupervised tool use [6]. InKHdiffers from these approaches in one crucial aspect: retrieval is not solely query-time decision made by the agent. Instead, relevant knowledge is injected into bounded working state before the next reasoning step.

sections.title: 3 Problem Formulation
sections.content: We model financial agent as operating over an event stream et E, where events may be user turns, tool observations, market updates, portfolio changes, or internally generated risk signals. workflow episode is wi = (cid:0)eti:ti+ℓi, ai,1:Hi, oi,1:Hi, yi where ai,h is an internal action such as retrieval or tool invocation, oi,h is the resulting observation, and yi is the user-visible output. sections.title: State and knowledge objects
sections.content: At time t, the agent maintains state St = (Ut, Mt, Rt, Xt, Gt), where Ut is user state, Mt is market state, Rt is risk state, Xt is workflow or execution state, and Gt is the temporal knowledge graph. Each knowledge object is represented as = (τ, σ, ϕ, ω, c, µ, ρ, tf , tl, ti). Here τ is the type, σ the scope, ϕ the content, ω the evidence and provenance, the confidence, µ the maturity, ρ the regime tag, tf the first-seen time, tl the last-validated time, and ti the invalidation time if the item has been superseded.

sections.title: Working context buffer
sections.content: Let denote detected active entities, intent, and risk class. Candidate knowledge is drawn from the graph neighborhood (Vt, ιt, χt) = Detect(et, St) Ct = Nh(Vt; Gt) {k : ti(k) t}, where Nh is the h-hop graph neighborhood and invalidated items are excluded. Each candidate receives utility st(k) = αrelRel(k, et)+αstrStruct(k, Vt)+αmatMat(µ(k))+αfreshFresh(k, t)+αregRegime(k, Mt)+αtrustTrust(k)αnoiseNoise(k). The injection operator under token budget is (cid:16) It = Compress TopB{st(k) : Ct At} (cid:17) , where At is the set of governance-admissible items. The working context buffer is then Ct = Fuse(Ut, Mt, Rt, Xt, It), Ct B. Here Ct denotes the bounded context assembled for the next reasoning step. This object is the online working state given to the agent for the next step. The conceptual difference from agent-driven retrieval is simple: the system prepares the context instead of forcing the model to search for it.

sections.title: Objective
sections.content: The agent is optimized not only for task performance but also for efficient, safe, and knowledgecompounding operation: Jt = E[Q(yt) λcCostt λrActRiskt + λkKnowGaint] . This objective formalizes the intended tradeoff between answer quality, serving cost, action risk, and future knowledge benefit. Effective confidence becomes ceff (k) = c(k) dt(k) [ti(k) = ]. Contradiction-triggered invalidation is handled explicitly: if new item contradicts prior item with score Γ(k, k), then Maturity evolves as Γ(k, k) > δ = ti(k) t. µt+1(k) = Ψ(cid:0)µt(k), νt(k), υt(k), qt(k), ht(k)(cid:1), where νt is reuse count, υt is validation evidence, qt is downstream utility attribution, and ht is human review.

sections.title: Governance constraints
sections.content: A knowledge item may influence financial action only if governance allows it: (cid:104) Allow(a, k) = ceff (k) ϵ µ(k) θ(risk(a)) σ(k) O(u) (cid:105) . Here O(u) is the permitted knowledge overlay for user and θ() is monotone threshold increasing with action risk.

sections.title: Theoretical propositions
sections.content: Proposition 1 (Passive injection versus agent-driven retrieval). Assume that solving task without reusable knowledge has expected cost C0. Let related prior knowledge yield expected savings > 0 with probability p. Let passive injection cost cp with irrelevant-context penalty ηp, and let agent-driven retrieval incur additional planning cost cℓ and penalty ηa. Then E[Cpassive] = C0 + cp + ηp, E[Cagent] = C0 + cp + cℓ + ηa. Hence passive injection is cheaper whenever Proof. Subtract the two expectations: cℓ > ηp ηa. E[Cagent] E[Cpassive] = cℓ + ηa ηp. If the extra planning or wiki-walk overhead exceeds the marginal irrelevant-context penalty of passive injection, passive injection is strictly cheaper in expectation. Proposition 2 (Governance reduces noise amplification). Suppose noisy memory items reproduce with effective branching factor β > 0, and governance suppresses fraction [0, 1] of noisy items before reuse. If Nt is the noisy memory mass at time t, then E[Nt+1 Nt] ζt + β(1 g)Nt, where ζt is fresh source noise. If β(1 g) < 1, noisy-memory growth is subcritical and remains bounded in expectation. Proof. The recurrence defines linear branching process with ratio β(1 g). If the ratio is less than one, the corresponding geometric series converges and expected noisy-memory mass remains bounded. Proposition 3 (Maturity gating for high-risk actions). Let the correctness probability of maturity state µ be πµ, and let action have benefit Ba when based on correct knowledge and loss La scaled by risk multiplier λa when based on incorrect knowledge. Then Using maturity level µ for action is rational only if EU (a, µ) = πµBa (1 πµ)λaLa. πµ λaLa Ba + λaLa . Because the right-hand side increases with λa, minimum acceptable maturity must rise with action risk. Proof. Rearrange the condition EU (a, µ) 0. Monotonicity in λa is immediate. 4 Interaction-Native Knowledge Harness

sections.title: Architecture
sections.content: Figure 1 shows the proposed system. The online path is interaction-native: every incoming event updates detected entities, retrieves compact, governed neighborhood from the temporal graph, and injects it into the working context buffer before the main agent step. The offline path is knowledge-native: completed workflows are extracted, upserted, invalidated when contradicted, and summarized into wiki audit surface. Graph as retrieval substrate, wiki as audit surface key practical design choice is to separate online retrieval from human-readable synthesis. The temporal graph is the retrieval substrate: it stores canonical entities, typed relations, provenance, last-validated time, and invalidation. The wiki is the audit surface: it stores readable asset pages, trader pages, strategies, risk notes, and maintenance logs. This division keeps online retrieval compact and cheap while preserving inspectability and human review [2, 19].

sections.title: Baseline comparison
sections.content: Table 1 contrasts the baselines used in this paper. 7 Figure 1: Interaction-native knowledge harness. The online path assembles governed low-latency context before the main agent step, while the offline path compiles workflow traces into persistent, auditable long-term knowledge. Table 1: Architectural components present in each baseline. sections.title: Algorithms
sections.content: Algorithm 1 Passive Injection Require: event et, state St, graph Gt, token budget B, hop radius 1: (Vt, ιt, χt) Detect(et, St) 2: Vt Canonicalize(Vt) 3: Ct Nh(Vt; Gt) 4: Ct RemoveInvalidated(Ct) 5: Ct FilterByGovernance(Ct, χt) 6: for all Ct do compute st(k) 7: 8: It Compress(TopB(Ct, st, B)) 9: return Ct Fuse(St, It) 8 Algorithm 2 Background Extraction and Upsert Require: completed workflow wi, graph Gt 1: Zi ExtractCandidateKnowledge(wi) 2: Zi AttachEvidenceAndTrust(Zi) 3: Zi MatchOrCreateCanonicalEntities(Zi, Gt) 4: Fi DetectContradictions(Zi, Gt) 5: MarkInvalidated(Fi) 6: Gt+1 Upsert(Gt, Zi) 7: UpdateWikiAuditPages(Zi, Fi) 8: return Gt+1 Algorithm 3 Maintenance Tick Require: temporal graph Gt 1: probe SampleProbeType() 2: RunStalenessSweep(Gt, probe) 3: RunLinkDiscoveryAndMergeChecks(Gt, probe) 4: RecomputeConfidenceAndMaturity(Gt) 5: AutoExecuteLowRiskFixes(Gt) 6: QueueHighImpactChangesForHumanReview(Gt) 7: return cleaned graph and maintenance log

sections.title: Implementation notes
sections.content: A production implementation should satisfy four engineering constraints. First, retrieval must be algorithmic and budgeted. For the common case, the system should inject compact context block without requiring an LLM-driven wiki walk. Second, entity matching must collapse aliases such as BTC, Bitcoin, and BTCUSDT into stable canonical entities. Third, invalidation must happen at write time when new evidence supersedes older knowledge. Fourth, the wiki remains essential, but chiefly as an audit surface rather than an online retrieval primitive.

sections.title: 5 Experimental Design
sections.content: Reported benchmark and public-data extension This paper reports results for controlled synthetic benchmark and also specifies, but does not execute, public-data replay extension. Table 2 distinguishes the two. The implemented artifact uses 24 seeds, 4 rounds, and 80 episodes per round. This yields 7,680 workflows per baseline and 46,080 baseline-conditioned evaluations in total. The four task families are market analysis, portfolio review, copy-trading evaluation, and trade preparation. Round 1 is cold start. Round 2 introduces user preference signals. Round 3 injects regime or protocol shocks. Round 4 measures post-shock reuse.

sections.title: Assumptions
sections.content: The reported suite is architecture-level rather than vendor-level. We therefore make four explicit assumptions: 9 Table 2: Evaluation stages. All reported quantitative results in this paper come from Stage A. Stage Status Data sources Purpose Reported Specified only Controlled synthetic workflows, simlatent preferulated user dialogues, ence signals, regime shocks, and staleknowledge traps Public market and filing replay built from FRED, SEC EDGAR, and Binance public market-data interfaces [27 29] plus simulated user dialogues Isolate architectural effects token cost, on latency, reuse, invalidation, and traceability Historical and human-scored extension for future work replay 1. A1 (Model abstraction). Baselines are modeled by characteristic token budgets, retrieval behavior, and latency distributions rather than tied to one proprietary API. 2. A2 (Cost abstraction). Token cost is modeled as $3.00 per million tokens plus $0.002 per tool call. 3. A3 (Latent ground truth). Task quality, stale-memory violations, and traceability are assessed against simulator-defined gold requirements. 4. A4 (Scope). Reported results validate system behavior, not live trading profitability.

sections.title: Baselines
sections.content: We compare six systems: ModelOnly, ToolAgent, SimpleMem, WikiWalk, Khnoinv, and the full InKH. The key comparison is between WikiWalk and InKH: both have compiled persistent knowledge, but only InKH uses passive injection and write-time invalidation.

sections.title: Metrics
sections.content: The main metrics are: Context precision = Repeated error reduction = gold hits max(1, retrieved items) (1 Q1) (1 QT ) 1 Q1 , , Cost efficiency = tokens/1000 . We also report latency, total tokens, stale-knowledge usage, decision traceability, and estimated serving cost.

sections.title: Main quantitative results
sections.content: Table 3 presents the central results. The full InKH baseline has the highest task quality and traceability among all systems, while also delivering lower latency than all nontrivial retrieval baselines. Relative to WikiWalk, InKH reduces latency by 82.95% and token load by 82.29%, while improving quality by 0.108, reducing stale-memory usage by 96.58%, and raising decision traceability by 0.461. Relative to SimpleMem, InKH improves quality by 0.157 and lowers stale usage by 96.02%. Relative to Khnoinv, InKH yields 0.050 increase in quality and 96.58% reduction in stale-memory usage with nearly identical token budget. Figure 2 visualizes the quality-latency frontier.

sections.title: Shock adaptation and repeated error reduction
sections.content: The most important empirical distinction appears after shocks are introduced in Round 3. Table 5 reports round-wise quality and repeated error reduction. Only the full InKH system improves materially from Round 1 to Round 4; all other baselines are flat or regress after shock introduction. Figure 3 makes the mechanism visible: after shocks, stale-memory usage spikes in all memory baselines except the full invalidation-enabled system. Task-family results Table 6 shows quality by task family. InKH is strongest across all four families and the largest gains are on the most operationally sensitive tasks: copy-trading evaluation and trade preparation. On high-risk workflows only (copy-trading and trade preparation plus shock-tagged episodes), InKH reaches quality 0.822, stale-memory usage 0.018, and traceability 0.999, compared with 0.766, 0.336, and 0.923 for Khnoinv, respectively. This is exactly where maturity and invalidation should matter most.

sections.title: Governance ablation
sections.content: A possible alternative explanation for the gains is simply that InKH stores more memory. Table 7 rules that out. Both InKH and Khnoinv accumulate essentially the same amount of knowledge and the same maturity mass. The difference is that InKH invalidates obsolete memory rather than letting it persist. This result is important for product design. It implies that better financial cognition does not come merely from remembering more. It comes from remembering under governance.

sections.title: What the results imply for real products
sections.content: Four product lessons follow directly from the experiments. Passive injection should replace wiki walking in the foreground path. The agent-driven In chat-like financial wiki baseline performs materially worse on both latency and token cost. 12 s d o - t 0.6 0.4 0.2 0 INKH KH-noinv Simple-memory Wiki-walk 2 3 4 Round Figure 3: Stale-knowledge usage by round. Write-time invalidation is the key differentiator after shocks. Table 6: Task-family quality by baseline. Task family InKH Khnoinv WikiWalk SimpleMem ToolAgent ModelOnly Copy-trading evaluation Market analysis Portfolio review Trade preparation 0.819 0.793 0.819 0.827 0.753 0.748 0.776 0. 0.693 0.693 0.718 0.723 0.657 0.665 0.675 0.633 0.537 0.556 0.538 0.531 0.437 0.456 0.437 0.431 workflows, the system must assemble context before the model reasons, not ask the model to perform multi-step search over its own memory. The graph should serve retrieval; the wiki should serve audit and review. This division preserves human interpretability without paying the full online cost of document-style traversal. Invalidation matters more than additional memory volume. Table 7 shows that the performance gap between InKH and Khnoinv arises from invalidation, not from more stored items. This is especially relevant in finance, where outdated assumptions can remain superficially plausible after regime breaks. The cognition plane and execution plane should be designed separately but coherently. Borjigin et al. show that execution safety must be enforced where side effects occur [25, 26]. Our results suggest an upstream complement: action survivability should be paired with cognition survivability. The user should not need to manually police the agents memory state, any more than they should manually police its last-mile execution permissions.

sections.title: Limitations
sections.content: This paper should be read with four limitations in mind. First, the reported evaluation is controlled synthetic benchmark. It is designed to isolate architecture-level propertieslatency, memory invalidation, reuse, and traceabilitynot live trading profitability. Second, the quality metric is simulator-defined rather than human-labeled. Third, the current artifact abstractly simulates graph-backed retrieval and serving behavior rather than instantiating full production graph database. Fourth, public-data replay using FRED, EDGAR, and Binance interfaces is specified but not yet reported. 13 Table 7: Knowledge inventory over 24 seeds. Both KH variants ingest the same amount of knowledge; only InKH invalidates obsolete items. Baseline Final items New items added Invalidated items Verified items Proven items Khnoinv InKH 13.96 13.96 5.96 5.96 0.00 2. 6.08 6.08 7.88 7.88 Accordingly, the right claim is not that InKH proves financial alpha. The right claim is narrower and more architectural: interaction-native, governed knowledge harnesses are better systems target for financial agents than turn-based retrieval plus disposable context.

sections.title: 8 Conclusion
sections.content: This paper introduced the interaction-native knowledge harness for continuous financial cognition. The central idea is simple: financial agent should not force the user to manage the systems cognitive complexity. Instead, the system should absorb that complexity by continuously maintaining structured state, injecting the right context at the right time, and transforming interaction traces into governed long-term knowledge. In reproducible benchmark, this design improves quality, lowers latency relative to nontrivial memory baselines, sharply reduces stale-memory usage, and substantially increases decision traceability. The strongest result is not merely that InKH remembers more. It is that InKH remembers under governance. The broader implication is practical. If future financial agents are to be adopted in real workflows, they will need both continuous cognition and survivable execution. This paper addresses the first requirement. Recent survivability-aware execution work addresses the second [25, 26]. Together they suggest more complete research program for financial AI: interaction-native cognition upstream, survivability-aware execution downstream.

sections.title: A Practical Engineering Appendix
sections.content: A.

sections.title: Implementation defaults
sections.content: Table 8 separates required architectural commitments from suggested defaults. The latter are implementation recommendations rather than claims of optimality. Table 8: Implementation defaults. Required means necessary for the claimed architecture; Suggested means recommended starting value. Setting Status Default Note Passive foreground injection Working context buffer budget Temporal graph memory Wiki audit surface Write-time invalidation Maturity gating Embedding dimension Structural candidate pool Hop radius Top returned items Simple-memory budget Wiki-walk budget Maintenance probe mix Required Required Required Required Required Required Suggested Suggested Suggested Suggested Suggested Suggested Suggested On 300600 kens Enabled Enabled Enabled InKH Central architectural feature Experiment default: 600 toCanonical entities + temporal edges Human-readable review layer Key governance feature in Strictest for high-risk workflows Vendor/model-agnostic default Enabled 1024 100300 edges Before reranking and compression 12 hops 1220 pact items Experiment baseline 1200 tokens 4200 tokens Experiment baseline 30/20/15/15/10/10Hub/link/quality/stale/merge/split Entity-neighborhood retrieval Before template compression comA.2 Synthetic benchmark mechanics The released artifact implements the benchmark as deterministic simulator with seed-controlled randomness. The quality function is: = clip(qb + βr(r 1) + βh hits βm missing βs stale + ϵ) , where qb is baseline-specific prior, is the round index, ϵ (0, σ2) with σ = 0.018, and βh, βs vary by baseline. For the KH variants, the retrieval-hit bonus is capped at 0.16 with coefficient 16 0.028. For WikiWalk it is capped at 0.13 with coefficient 0.024. For SimpleMem it is capped at 0.12 with coefficient 0.022. Missing gold requirements incur penalty of 0.02 per miss. Stale-memory use incurs penalty of 0.11 for WikiWalk, SimpleMem, and Khnoinv, and 0.04 for the full InKH. A.3 Data schemas minimal raw evidence record: { } { } { } "chunk_id": "raw_000123", "source_type": "market_api", "source_ref": "binance:BTCUSDT:1h", "timestamp": "2026-05-13T08:00:00Z", "trust_tier": "high", "content": "... immutable source payload ..." minimal entity record: "entity_id": "asset:BTC", "canonical_name": "BTC", "aliases": ["Bitcoin", "XBT", "BTCUSDT"], "entity_type": "ASSET", "summary": "Primary crypto asset used as liquidity anchor.", "updated_at": "2026-05-13T08:05:00Z" minimal edge record: "edge_id": "edge_004512", "src": "asset:BTC", "dst": "risk:slippage", "relation_type": "affected_by", "description": "Observed slippage rises under high volatility.", "evidence_ids": ["raw_000123", "trace_000045"], "confidence": 0.83, "maturity": "verified", "regime_tag": "high_volatility", "valid_at": "2026-05-10T00:00:00Z", "invalid_at": null, "updated_at": "2026-05-13T08:05:00Z" A.4 Reproduction The released artifact contains configuration files, simulator code, synthetic data, result tables, and figure-generation scripts. To reproduce the synthetic benchmark, run: python scripts/run_synthetic_suite.py This regenerates per-workflow logs, summary tables, confidence intervals, paired tests, and result figures.
